title: Chelsea Top Bunk
summary: A twin-size top bunk with jigsaw-arched headers, sized to stand on its own 2x2 legs or bolt down onto a bookshelf or desk base.
description:
This top bunk is designed to work two ways: freestanding on its own four 2x2 legs, or screwed down from underneath onto a separate bookshelf or desk unit that becomes the "bottom" of the bunk system. Either way, the two arched headers are the identity of the piece — cut the first with a jigsaw, clamp it to the second and sand both together until the curves match exactly, the same trick used on any paired curved part.

This is a real bed a child sleeps on top of, several feet off the ground, so every structural connection here matters more than on a typical shelf project: solid cleats every 6–10", full-length guardrails with gaps filled rather than left open, and — if you're bolting this onto a separate base rather than building the included legs — a base that's genuinely rated to carry a loaded mattress and sleeper, not just holding up books.

tools.note: Cuts the arched headers — cut one, then use it as a template for the second.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, particle board, or MDF, half sheet
1x3 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Slats and guardrails — adjust quantity to your actual needs.

cutList.note: Cut an arched profile with a jigsaw; sand the pair together until they match.
You may want these shorter, depending on your access needs.

steps.title: Cut and match the headers
steps.body: Mark and carefully cut both headers with a jigsaw — cutting 1-1/2" stock with a jigsaw takes patience, so go slow and give yourself room to correct small errors. Clamp both headers together once cut and sand the top edges until they match perfectly, working from coarse to medium grit.

steps.title: Attach headers to panels
steps.body: If using a pocket-hole jig, drill all pocket holes before assembly. Without one, use the 2x2 legs to hold the header in place temporarily while you glue and clamp the header to the panel. Attach headers to both panels this way.
materials: 3/4" plywood, particle board, or MDF, half sheet

steps.title: Attach the panel bases
steps.body: Attach the base trim to the outside of each panel using 1-1/4" finish nails and glue.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: If not using pocket holes, screw through the legs into the header with 3" screws (predrilled and countersunk) and apply ample glue along the header's bottom edge. Attach the legs to the panels using countersunk screws from outside the legs, or the pocket holes drilled earlier.

steps.title: Build the guardrails
steps.body: Attach the guardrail boards to the guardrail legs with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue (or screws), adjusting carefully for square as you go — square is the one thing that's genuinely tricky about an otherwise simple assembly. Fill any remaining gaps with scrap 1x3.

steps.title: Add cleats and slats
steps.body: Attach the cleats flush to the bottom with 2" screws and glue, placing screws every 6–10". If not using a bunkie board, lay the slats across the cleats and screw down.

steps.title: Attach to the base
steps.body: If pairing this with a separate bookshelf or desk base, screw the bunk down to the base from underneath. Confirm the base is genuinely rated to carry the loaded weight of a mattress and sleeper before use — this is a real structural connection, not decorative.

images.alt: Twin-size top bunk bed with arched headers and full guardrails
